macro_segments.title: Introduction to Seder Taharos and Masechet Niddah: Scope and Relevance

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on Seder Taharos and the unique status of Masechet Niddah as the only masechet in Taharos with a Gemara
The dual nature of Niddah: tumah v'taharah and the prohibition of sexual relations (taharas hamishpacha)
Distinction between the pure tumah v'taharah concerns and the practical halachic implications relevant today
The relationship between tumah status and the prohibition of sexual relations; investigation of whether they are always linked

macro_segments.title: The First Mishnah: Shammai's Position on Retroactive Tumah and the Principle of Chazakah

micro_segments.title: Shammai's ruling: 'Kol Hanashim Dayan Sha'atan' – all women, their moment suffices; meaning only from the moment of discovery is she tamai
The concept of 'isha mitama mibifnim kimibachutz' – a woman becomes tamai even while blood is interior to her body, before external exit
Practical implications: when a woman touched terumah in the morning and later discovers her period, the question becomes whether the terumah is tahor or tamei
The underlying halachic principle: chazakat taharah (presumption of purity) establishes a woman in her prior status until definitively proven otherwise
Shammai's two-fold reasoning: the principle of chazakah and the assumption that blood exits the body immediately, not delayed in internal passages

macro_segments.title: Hillel's Position and the Rabbis' Middle Ground: Retroactive Tumah within Time Limits

micro_segments.title: Hillel's position: 'mipkuda lipkuda' – from checking to checking, even over many days, a woman is retroactively tamai
Practical observance of checking: women checked twice daily (morning and evening) to limit the retroactive window of concern
The Rabbis' compromise: neither Shammai's nor Hillel's extreme, but rather limited by the lesser of 24 hours or the last checking
The distinction between women with a regular veset (period) versus other women; only regular women have the special leniency of 'daya sha'atah'
Critical principle: even under the Rabbis' retroactive concern, a woman counts her seven days of niddah only from the day she actually saw blood, not from the earliest retroactive possibility

macro_segments.title: Gemara Analysis: Shammai's Reasoning and Hillel's Rebuttal Regarding Chazakah

micro_segments.title: Mai ta'ama d'Shammai: Shammai establishes the woman on her chazakah (presumption of purity), assuming her status remained unchanged until the last moment
Hillel's counterargument: chazakah does not apply when there is a rei'usa migufa – an inherent reason for status change emanating from the body itself
Interpretation of rei'usa migufa: either as chazakah asuyah lehishtanot (a status inherently subject to change) or as competing chazakot (past purity vs. present tamei)

macro_segments.title: Gemara Comparison: Mikvah Case (Metame Mafrea) and Its Distinction from the Niddah Case

micro_segments.title: Introduction of a parallel case: a mikvah measured and found lacking retroactively taints all items and people immersed in it
The mikvah case renders items definitely tamei (vodai), whereas in the niddah case the retroactive concern is only a safek; the Gemara explores why the cases differ
The mikvah case involves both reshut harabbim and reshut hayachid; unclear why the niddah case does not discuss private vs. public domain distinction
Key distinction: the mikvah case involves an object starting with definite tamei status (the immersing person or vessel) trying to change status via a questionable mikvah
Biological discussion: unlike the constantly draining mikvah, the woman does not slowly bleed throughout the month; blood accumulates and then exits
Final conclusion: the mikvah case has two problems (chaseir mikvah AND definite tamei status of immersing object), while niddah has only one (the woman's current status); hence mikvah case is worse

macro_segments.title: Gemara Comparison: Wine Barrel Case (Chavis) and Its Parallel to the Mikvah and Niddah Cases

micro_segments.title: The barrel case: wine designated for terumah retroactively becomes questionable (safek) when discovered to have turned to vinegar
The question of wine transformation: the first three days are definite, thereafter a safek; what changed and when?
Parallel to mikvah: the barrel case is difficult for Shammai because retroactively the wine's status is questioned, yet it should be treated like the mikvah case
The complication: in the barrel case there is also a definite tevel (improperly tithed wine) whose status you are trying to change, making it structurally similar to the mikvah case
The Gemara back-and-forth: exploring whether the barrel case should yield safek or vadai, and whether it matches the mikvah case
Resolution: Rabbi Chanina of Sura identifies the barrel case as authored by Rabbi Shimon, who holds even the mikvah case yields only safek, not vadai

Final principle: when there are competing chazakot and a questionable middle state, whether that safek can change another object's definite status depends on whether one holds like Rabbi Shimon or the Chachamim

topical_tags.term: Isha mitama mibifnim (internal tumah before external bleeding)
Chazakah asuyah lehishtanot (status inherently subject to change)
Rei'usa migufa (inherent reason for status change from the body)
Reshut harabbim and reshut hayachid (public and private domain)
Isur arayot (prohibition of forbidden sexual relations)
